search_results.title: OWASP Secure Headers Project
search_results.snippet: The OWASP Secure Headers Project (also called OSHP) describes HTTP response headers that your application can use to increase the security of your application.

message.content:
The **OWASP Secure Headers Project** and the **OWASP HTTP Headers Cheat Sheet** overlap, but they are not identical: the Secure Headers Project is the more explicit inventory of recommended response headers, while the cheat sheet adds guidance for several legacy or non-security headers such as `Server`, `X-Powered-By`, `X-AspNet-Version`, and `Set-Cookie` attributes.[1][2] Below is an exhaustive, practical enumeration of the headers covered across those two OWASP sources, including the ones you explicitly requested and the closely related removal/attribute guidance from the cheat sheet.[1][2]

> Canonical project page: **OWASP Secure Headers Project** at `owasp.org/www-project-secure-headers/`.[1]  
> Canonical cheat sheet page: **HTTP Headers - OWASP Cheat Sheet Series** at `cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html`.[2]

| Header | OWASP-recommended value | Protects against / purpose | Common misconfigurations to test for | Deprecated? | Canonical OWASP URL |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strict-Transport-Security** | `max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload`.[2] | Forces browsers to use HTTPS, reducing SSL stripping / downgrade attacks.[2] | Missing `includeSubDomains`; short `max-age`; setting before all subdomains are HTTPS-ready; omitting `preload` if you intend HSTS preload; sending over HTTP instead of HTTPS.[2] | **No**.[1][2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **Content-Security-Policy** | OWASP gives CSP guidance rather than a single universal value; the cheat sheet emphasizes a policy that disables inline JavaScript and otherwise tightly restricts sources.[2] | Mitigates XSS and unauthorized resource loading by restricting where scripts, styles, images, etc. may load from.[2] | Using `*`; allowing `unsafe-inline`; allowing `unsafe-eval`; missing `default-src`; allowing overly broad third-party domains; policy not enforced in report-only only.[2] | **No**.[1][2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **X-Frame-Options** | `DENY`.[2] | Prevents clickjacking by blocking rendering in frames/iframes/embeds/objects.[2] | Using `ALLOWALL` or equivalent via framework defaults; conflicting with CSP `frame-ancestors`; using `SAMEORIGIN` where third-party framing is possible.[2] | **No**.[1][2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **X-Content-Type-Options** | `nosniff`.[2] | Prevents MIME sniffing, reducing drive-by script execution and content-type confusion.[2] | Missing on HTML/JS/CSS responses; incorrect `Content-Type`; relying on extension rather than MIME type; applying only to some routes.[2] | **No**.[1][2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **Referrer-Policy** | `strict-origin-when-cross-origin`.[2] | Limits leakage of full URLs in the `Referer` header.[2] | Using `unsafe-url`; using `no-referrer-when-downgrade` on sensitive apps; inconsistent policy across pages; assuming it protects against all referrer leakage.[2] | **No**.[1][2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **Permissions-Policy** | OWASP Secure Headers lists it as a working draft header; no single stable recommended value is provided in the cited material.[1] | Restricts access to browser features such as camera, microphone, geolocation, etc.[1] | Leaving powerful features enabled by default; using malformed directives; allowing unwanted origins; forgetting that browser support and syntax differ from older `Feature-Policy` behavior.[1] | **No**, but OWASP marks it as **working draft** in the project page.[1] | `https://owasp.org/www-project-secure-headers/` |
| **Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy** | `same-origin`.[2] | Isolates browsing contexts to same-origin documents, helping mitigate cross-window data leaks and some cross-origin side-channel issues.[2] | Using `unsafe-none`; setting it only on some endpoints; breaking legitimate popups or integrations; not pairing with related cross-origin isolation headers when required.[2] | **No**.[1][2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy** | OWASP Secure Headers includes it as an active header, but the cited cheat sheet excerpt does not provide a single recommended value in the supplied results.[1][2] | Helps enforce cross-origin isolation for embedding and is commonly used with COOP for powerful web platform features and isolation.[1][2] | Using a value that blocks needed third-party resources; inconsistent deployment across assets; forgetting resource CORS/CORP requirements.[1][2] | **No**.[1] | `https://owasp.org/www-project-secure-headers/` |
| **Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy** | `same-site`.[2] | Limits which sites can load a resource, reducing unauthorized cross-site inclusion and some data exfiltration risks.[2] | Setting `cross-origin` unnecessarily; not aligning with actual resource-sharing requirements; breaking legitimate CDN or federated usage.[2] | **No**.[1][2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **Cache-Control** | OWASP guidance is contextual: use `no-store` for sensitive data; `private` may be used when caching is limited to non-shared caches.[2] The Secure Headers project lists `Cache-Control` as active but the supplied result does not show one universal value.[1][2] | Prevents sensitive responses from being stored in browser or intermediary caches.[2] | Leaving defaults; omitting `no-store` on sensitive pages; using `private` when shared caches must also be prevented; forgetting related headers such as `Pragma`/`Expires` where legacy clients matter.[2] | **No**.[1][2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **Clear-Site-Data** | OWASP lists it as active, but the supplied result does not show a single recommended directive set.[1][2] | Clears browser data such as cache, cookies, storage, and execution contexts when used during logout or incident response.[1][2] | Using overly broad directives that wipe more state than intended; sending on unrelated responses; failing to pair with proper session invalidation server-side.[1][2] | **No**.[1] | `https://owasp.org/www-project-secure-headers/` |
| **X-Permitted-Cross-Domain-Policies** | OWASP lists it as active; the usual secure posture is to disable Flash/FDF policy loading, typically with a restrictive or absent policy depending on platform guidance.[1][2] | Prevents Adobe cross-domain policy files from authorizing unintended cross-domain data access.[1][2] | Leaving permissive policy values; retaining support for legacy Flash workflows; inconsistent placement on content-serving responses.[1][2] | **No**.[1] | `https://owasp.org/www-project-secure-headers/` |
| **X-XSS-Protection** | `0`.[2] | Legacy browser XSS filter control; OWASP recommends disabling it because modern browsers deprecated it and it can introduce issues.[2] | Leaving `1; mode=block`; assuming it protects against XSS; relying on it instead of CSP; not recognizing browser support differences.[2] | **Yes, legacy/deprecated** in practice; OWASP recommends not using it and explicitly turning it off.[2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **Server** | Remove it or use a non-informative value; OWASP shows a non-specific example such as `webserver`.[2] The project page also includes `Server` among headers to remove/reduce.[1][2] | Reduces server/software fingerprinting.[1][2] | Leaking exact product/version strings; exposing OS and module details; different values across environments; assuming obfuscation is a substitute for patching.[2] | **No**, but OWASP recommends minimizing or removing it.[1][2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **X-Powered-By** | Remove it.[2] | Reduces technology fingerprinting by hiding framework/runtime details.[2] | Leaving framework defaults enabled; exposing PHP, ASP.NET, Express, or other stack identifiers; stripping it at one layer but re-adding it upstream.[2] | **No**, but OWASP recommends removal.[2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **X-AspNet-Version** | Disable sending this header.[2] | Reduces ASP.NET version disclosure / fingerprinting.[2] | Leaving it enabled in IIS/ASP.NET; confusing it with `X-AspNetMvc-Version`; removing only at the app layer but not server configuration.[2] | **No**, but OWASP recommends disabling/removing it.[2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |
| **Set-Cookie** attributes | OWASP’s cookie guidance is to use security attributes such as `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, and appropriate `SameSite`; the exact value depends on the cookie’s purpose and transport requirements.[2] | Helps protect session cookies from theft via JavaScript, MITM on non-HTTPS, and cross-site request abuse.[2] | Missing `Secure`; missing `HttpOnly` on session cookies; lax `SameSite`; overly broad `Domain` or `Path`; long-lived session cookies; setting sensitive cookies over HTTP.[2] | **No**.[2] | `https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet.html` |

A few important scope notes:

- The **Secure Headers Project** page explicitly marks `Permissions-Policy` as a **working draft** and lists these headers as **active**: `Strict-Transport-Security`, `X-Frame-Options`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Permitted-Cross-Domain-Policies`, `Referrer-Policy`, `Clear-Site-Data`, `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy`, `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy`, `Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy`, `Cache-Control`, and `X-DNS-Prefetch-Control`.[1]  
- The supplied OWASP project excerpt also shows a **removal** table containing headers such as `Server`, which aligns with the cheat sheet’s advice to remove or de-inform `Server`, `X-Powered-By`, and `X-AspNet-Version`.[1][2]  
- The cheat sheet’s `Content-Security-Policy` guidance is principle-based and does **not** prescribe one universal policy string, because the correct policy depends on the application’s resources and trust boundaries.[2]  
- For `Set-Cookie`, OWASP treats security as a matter of cookie attributes rather than a single header value; the right attributes depend on whether the cookie must be sent cross-site, only over TLS, or inaccessible to JavaScript.[2]  

If you want, I can turn this into a **QA test matrix** with columns for “expected present,” “expected absent,” “negative test,” and “example curl/burp assertions” for each header.
